AIntuition Collection: A Utility-Focused NFT Ecosystem Designed for the Next Era of Web3
Source: CoinGape    Published: 2026-03-16 07:31:46

Buy on Mexc Buy on Bitget Play. Earn. Earn $GGs. On Base. The blockchain industry continues to redefine how people interact with digital assets. What began as an experiment with decentralized currencies has evolved into a broad ecosystem that includes smart contracts, decentralized applications, and non-fungible tokens (NFTs). In recent years, NFTs have expanded beyond digital art to become tools for access, membership, and participation in online platforms. The AIntuition Collection represents this new direction by introducing NFTs that unlock a range of exclusive privileges within a growing ecosystem. The project aims to demonstrate how NFTs can function as functional digital assets rather than simple collectibles. By combining scarcity, rewards, and platform privileges, the AIntuition Collection offers holders an opportunity to participate in a community-driven Web3 environment. Scarcity plays an important role in determining the value and exclusivity of NFT projects. The AIntuition Collection has been designed with a strict supply cap of 15,000 NFTs, ensuring that the ecosystem remains limited and exclusive. Rather than releasing the entire collection at once, the project is introducing NFTs gradually through multiple seasons. The first season includes 5,000 NFTs, providing early participants with the opportunity to join the platform before future releases expand the supply. Each NFT is priced at 250 USDC, creating a clear and transparent entry point for collectors and Web3 enthusiasts who want to become part of the ecosystem. This phased launch strategy helps maintain balance between accessibility and scarcity, allowing the project to build its community organically. One of the most engaging aspects of the AIntuition Collection is its mystery chest purchasing system. Instead of purchasing an NFT with a predetermined rarity level, users receive a sealed digital chest containing an unrevealed NFT. The rarity of the NFT remains hidden until the holder chooses to open the chest. Once opened, the NFT randomly reveals one of three rarity levels: The distribution of these NFTs in the first season is carefully structured: This reveal mechanism adds a gamified element to the collection process. Buyers do not know which rarity they will receive until the moment they open their chest, creating an experience similar to discovering a rare digital collectible. Participation in the AIntuition Collection is designed to be accessible and user-friendly. Users begin by connecting their crypto wallet to OpenSea, one of the largest marketplaces for NFTs. After connecting their wallet, they can purchase one of the available NFT chests. Once the transaction is completed, the chest appears in the user's wallet. At any time, the holder can open the chest to reveal the rarity of the NFT. After the NFT is revealed, it can be linked to the user's account within the AIntuition platform, where it activates a range of privileges and rewards. A key feature of the AIntuition ecosystem is its integration between NFT ownership and platform functionality.Once the NFT is revealed, the holder connects their wallet to their AIntuition account. The platform then activates benefits according to the rarity level of the NFT. To maintain fairness and transparency, the platform performs daily automated wallet checks. If the NFT is transferred or sold, the privileges associated with it are automatically disabled. This system ensures that rewards remain tied directly to NFT ownership. The AIntuition Collection features a three-tier reward structure designed to offer increasing levels of access and support. Bronze NFTs represent the most common tier but still offer meaningful benefits. Bronze holders receive: This tier provides an accessible entry point into the AIntuition ecosystem. Silver NFTs offer a higher level of service and engagement. Silver holders receive: This tier is designed for users who want more personalized interaction with the platform. Gold NFTs represent the most exclusive membership level in the collection. With only 500 NFTs available, they include premium privileges such as: These NFTs offer the most comprehensive experience within the AIntuition ecosystem. One of the key goals of the AIntuition Collection is to build a strong and engaged community. All NFT holders gain access to a private club environment where they can interact with other members of the ecosystem. This space is designed to encourage discussion, collaboration, and networking among participants. Gold NFT holders gain additional opportunities through offline events that bring the digital community together in real-world settings. The AIntuition Collection highlights a growing trend in the blockchain industry: the rise of utility-focused NFTs. Instead of existing purely as collectible items, these NFTs function as access keys that unlock benefits and opportunities within digital ecosystems. By combining scarcity, gamified discovery, token rewards, and community engagement, the AIntuition Collection demonstrates how NFTs can evolve into powerful tools for participation in Web3 platforms. As the NFT market continues to mature, projects like AIntuition may help define the next generation of blockchain-based communities. Our journal analysts bring years of experience in market analysis and blockchain technology to ensure factual accuracy and balanced reporting. By following our Editorial Policy, our writers verify every source, fact-check each story, rely on reputable sources, and attribute quotes and media correctly. We also follow a rigorous Review Methodology when evaluating exchanges and tools. From emerging blockchain projects and coin launches to industry events and technical developments, we cover all facets of the digital asset space with unwavering commitment to timely, relevant information. US Senate Votes to Ban CBDCs, Promoting Dollar-backed Stablecoins Instead
Source: East & Partners    Published: 2026-03-16 02:49:35

East & Partners (17 March 2026 – United States) On Thursday, 12 March, the US Senate voted in favour of the central bank digital currency (CBDC) amendment, prohibiting the Federal Reserve from issuing a CBDC until 2030. The amendment passed with an 89-10 vote, receiving strong bipartisan support across the Senate. The restriction is embedded within the broader 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act, which aims to address housing affordability in the United States. However, the future of the legislation remains uncertain as it moves to the House of Representatives. US President Donald Trump has indicated that he may refuse to sign any new laws unless Congress first passes a requirement that voters must prove their identification and citizenship before partaking in this year's congressional midterm election. A CBDC is a digital form of a nation's sovereign currency, issued and regulated directly by the national bank. Opposition to a US CBDC has long been popular among Republican lawmakers, many of whom argue that a government-issued digital currency could threaten financial privacy. Back in January, when he signed an executive order to prevent federal agencies from issuing CBDCs, Trump said: “Measures will be taken to protect Americans from the risks of CBDCs, which threaten financial system stability, individual privacy, and US sovereignty. This includes prohibiting the establishment, issuance, and use of CBDCs within the United States.” The key provisions of the new order also include advancing the global growth of dollar-backed stablecoins, protecting access to open blockchain networks, promoting fair access to banking services, and creating a ‘President's Working Group on Digital Asset Markets'. “Financial privacy is a cornerstone of American freedom, and any decision to authorise a CBDC must remain with Congress and the American people,” said Cody Carbone, CEO of the Digital Chamber. “We appreciate the Senate reinforcing that digital innovation in the United States should be led by the private sector while protecting individual liberty.” Yet, although most stablecoins like Tether and USD Coin are pegged to the US dollar, private stablecoins may struggle to reach global dominance with the efficiency which a centrally-led CBDC can. The new development could impact the dollar's global dominance, as other sovereign digital currencies gain prominence. For instance, the Chinese digital yuan is already being tested, alongside aims to create Chinese alternatives to the Belgium-based SWIFT system. In November 2025, China and the UAE conducted their first cross-border payment using the mBridge platform, which connects different countries' CBDCs on a shared ledger. It was a demonstration transaction, with each party using its own CBDC (digital dirham and digital yuan). The mBridge platform handled the instant FX conversion and settlement between them without traditional banking intermediaries. The digital yuan currently dominates on the mBridge network, accounting for around 95% of transaction volumes. Source: Trade Finance Global At East & Partners we work together as one firm to serve our clients wherever they need us. Our collective knowledge and experience across global markets helps us guide clients on the intricacies of each region while enabling cohesion across their global footprint. Apples with apples and pears with pears in complex and demanding financial services marketsglobally. Δ The intelligence of leading decision-makers

SCIENCE

Widespread presence of anthropogenic compounds in marine dissolved organic matter
Source: Nature    Published: 2026-03-16 16:56:05

Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles and JavaScript. Advertisement Nature Geoscience (2026)Cite this article Chemical pollution has profound impacts on marine ecosystem health and services. Most investigations of the distributions of anthropogenic organic chemicals (xenobiotics) have been regionally focused, which limits our understanding of the extent of chemical pollution in the world's largest biome. To address this gap, we mapped the presence of xenobiotics across marine ecosystems. Here we present a meta-analysis of 21 public non-targeted tandem mass spectrometry datasets, which are not restricted to a predefined set of compounds but rather capture thousands of chemicals. These datasets comprise 2,315 seawater samples, spanning coastal to open ocean environments across three ocean basins. Our analysis revealed that common pollutants such as pesticides and pharmaceuticals were predominantly detected in estuaries and coastal areas but declined with distance from shore, whereas industrial chemicals and additives, including polyalkylene glycols, phthalates and organophosphates, were widely distributed across marine ecosystems. A total of 248 annotated xenobiotic features contributed a median of 2% to the total detected peak area per sample. We observed highest median levels of xenobiotic contribution in coastal datasets (up to 20%) and lowest levels (0.5%) in open ocean datasets, which indicates that anthropogenic organic substances contribute substantially to the dissolved organic matter pool in the surface ocean. All processed data and mzmine batch files used in this study are available via Zenodo at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.16687158 (ref. 51). Source data are provided with this paper. All code, including python scripts, is available via Github at https://github.com/Functional-Metabolomics-Lab/Marine-Xenobiotics and via Zenodo at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.16687158 (ref. 51). Construction Workers Were Restoring a Historic City—and Uncovered a Roman Tunnel Beneath the Streets
Source: Popular Mechanics    Published: 2026-03-16 12:30:00

Its design is extremely rare for the region where it was found. Gear-obsessed editors choose every product we review. We may earn commission if you buy from a link. Why Trust Us? Here's what you'll learn when you read this story: Experts had no idea that a large Roman-era water channel was hiding beneath the historic streets of Trabzon, Türkiye. But when construction crews working on a restoration project in the city center came upon a nearly 1,300-foot-long tunnel made of interlocking stones, archeologists were quick to take notice. Dated to roughly the second century C.E., the tunnel's original purpose was likely to supply freshwater to ships at the ancient harbor on the Black Sea. The construction methods used to build the tunnel make it unique in the region, but easily recognizable to connoisseurs of ancient Roman craftsmanship. And, after nearly 2,000 years, the thing still works. The tunnel was uncovered about six feet below ground by construction crews working on a modernization project in Trabzon's Comlekci neighborhood, a short distance uphill from the Roman-era port. The structure measures roughly 4 by 4 feet and features small walkways on both sides. The vaulted tunnel features an arched roof, which was typical of Roman-era design, but highly unusual for any area of Roman-era Anatolia. The subterranean channel is principally made from finely-cut stone, reinforced by rubble stone and a layer of plaster meant to serve as waterproofing, according to a report from the Hurriyet Daily News. “The discovery came as a surprise,” said Mehmet Yavuz, dean of the Faculty of Letters and head of the Art History Department at Karadeniz Technical University. “We did not previously consider that area to have been an intensely inhabited zone during the Roman or Hellenistic periods.” In another twist, the tunnel isn't just a straight shot from the start to end. Instead, it features winding turns, steps, and bends—possibly designed to slow the flow of the water. Over the course of nearly two millennia, successive alterations changed the channel's shape and purpose. New openings made in the walls during the Ottoman or early Republican periods allowed the fresh-water tunnel to turn into a wastewater conveyance system, and wastewater runoff still flows through the stone canal today. Yavuz said that the construction style of the tunnel—including the craftsmanship and materials used to construct it—places it within the early Roman period from the late first to early second centuries C.E. It may well have been commissioned during the rule of the emperors Trajan or Hadrian, and it's possible that it was part of a larger aqueduct system connecting Trabzon to inland cities where Roman soldiers were garrisoned. The team of researchers from Trabzon Museum Directorate and Karadeniz Technical University hopes to continue exploring, according to Türkiye Today, as they believe that there is a possibility of locating additional connections to other tunnels or key above-ground sites. Though some sections have already been destroyed by construction activity above, as they search the length of the tunnel, experts could uncover more clues about its history and use. Tim Newcomb is a journalist based in the Pacific Northwest. He covers stadiums, sneakers, gear, infrastructure, and more for a variety of publications, including Popular Mechanics. His favorite interviews have included sit-downs with Roger Federer in Switzerland, Kobe Bryant in Los Angeles, and Tinker Hatfield in Portland. Just 24 minutes of specially designed music could significantly reduce anxiety
Source: ScienceDaily    Published: 2026-03-16 11:05:22

A new randomized clinical trial suggests that listening to specially designed music for just 24 minutes may significantly lower anxiety. The music was paired with auditory beat stimulation (ABS), a technique that uses rhythmic sound patterns to influence brain activity. Researchers say the approach could provide an accessible, drug free option for people looking for additional ways to manage stress and regulate emotions. The clinical trial was conducted by psychology researchers Danielle K. Mullen and Frank A. Russo at Toronto Metropolitan University (TMU) in partnership with LUCID, a digital therapeutics company that emerged from TMU's Zone Learning ecosystem. Music Based Therapy as a Potential Anxiety Tool Anxiety affects millions of people around the world. Common treatments include medications and cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), but these options can come with challenges such as side effects, long wait times, cost, and the time required for ongoing treatment. Because of these barriers, researchers have been exploring music based digital therapeutics as a low cost and widely accessible way to help people manage anxiety symptoms. These tools aim to deliver quick relief through guided listening experiences that can be used almost anywhere. Clinical Trial Tested Different Listening Lengths The study included 144 adults who had moderate trait anxiety and were already taking medication to help manage their symptoms. Participants were randomly assigned to one of four listening conditions: Before and after the listening sessions, participants completed standardized assessments that measured anxiety levels and mood. Researchers Identify a 24 Minute Sweet Spot The results showed that listening to music with ABS significantly reduced both cognitive and somatic symptoms of anxiety compared with the pink noise control. Participants also reported improvements in negative mood. Among the different listening durations tested, the 24 minute session produced the strongest overall reduction in anxiety. Its effects were similar to the 36 minute session and clearly stronger than the 12 minute session. "What we're seeing is a dose-response pattern where about 24 minutes of music with ABS seems to be the sweet spot," said Russo, Professor of Psychology at TMU and Chief Science Officer, LUCID. "It's long enough to meaningfully shift anxiety levels, but not so long that listeners need to carve out a large block of time." "Investigating the dose-response relationship between music and anxiety reduction: A randomized clinical trial" was published in PLOS Mental Health on January 21, 2026. Story Source: Materials provided by Toronto Metropolitan University. Note: Content may be edited for style and length. Scientists discover what really happens during sourdough fermentation
Source: ScienceDaily    Published: 2026-03-16 11:00:43

Bread has been a dietary staple for thousands of years, and sourdough is experiencing a renewed surge in popularity. Many people value it for its natural ingredients, nutritional benefits, and distinctive flavor. Yet the biological processes behind sourdough fermentation remain surprisingly complex. What exactly occurs during this fermentation, and how do wheat fibers influence the final quality of bread? These questions guided the doctoral research of Víctor González Alonso at the Vrije Universiteit Brussel (VUB), who investigated how specific wheat dietary fibers called arabinoxylans behave during sourdough fermentation. "Wheat provides a large share of the calories and fiber consumed in Europe," González Alonso explains, "and arabinoxylans play an important part in this. They help determine the structure and quality of bread." Wheat Fibers and Sourdough Fermentation Arabinoxylans (AX) exist in two main forms. Water-extractable (WE-AX) typically have either beneficial or neutral effects on dough, while water-unextractable (WU-AX) can negatively influence bread quality. Until recently, scientists had limited understanding of how the microorganisms in sourdough interact with these fibers. To explore this question, González Alonso examined fermentation in several flour types, including some enriched with additional AX. He tracked changes in the microbial populations using advanced DNA analysis and metabolite profiling to better understand how fermentation unfolds. "We observed that sourdoughs develop into stable microbial ecosystems, with lactic acid bacteria and yeasts in a complex balance," he says. "A higher fiber content barely altered this process, although we clearly demonstrated that sourdough fermentation converts part of the WE-AX into WU-AX." Enzymes and Microbes Shape Bread Flavor One unexpected finding involved the source of this fiber transformation. The change was driven less by the bacteria themselves and more by enzymes already present in the wheat. These enzymes become active as the dough grows more acidic during fermentation. When this happens, large fiber molecules break down into smaller fragments, which may influence both the digestibility and the texture of the bread. The researchers also identified bacteria that contribute to flavor development. Lactococcus lactis was associated with buttery aromas, while Limosilactobacillus fermentum produced sugar alcohols that can add a mild sweetness. Testing the Findings in Real Bread The team extended their work beyond laboratory experiments. In a pilot scale baking trial, bread was made using wheat flour enriched with high levels of AX. The resulting sourdough loaves were not only higher in nutritional value but also showed a broader range of flavors. "Sourdough remains a fascinating interplay of biology and craftsmanship," González Alonso concludes. "And our research shows that sourdough fermentation influences wheat fibers to a greater extent than previously thought." Story Source: Materials provided by Vrije Universiteit Brussel (VUB). Note: Content may be edited for style and length. Cite This Page: New Research Reveals a Simple Supplement May Slow Biological Aging Chimps Seem To Love Crystals. A battery-free smart mask for long-term exhaled breath biochemical sensing
Source: Nature    Published: 2026-03-16 10:37:23

Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles and JavaScript. Advertisement Nature Sensors (2026)Cite this article Exhaled breath condensate (EBC) offers a non-invasive window into respiratory and systemic metabolism, yet wearable EBC systems are constrained by unstable moisture harvesting, short sensor lifetimes in humidity and battery dependence. Here we present EBClite, a battery-free smart mask for multiday EBC biomarker monitoring, using lactate as a model analyte. The platform integrates a regenerable antidrying porous hydrogel that achieves sustained and reactivatable breath condensation over several days, extending operational lifetime while reducing material cost. EBClite further incorporates a long-term stable electrochemical lactate sensor and an ultrathin quasi-two-dimensional perovskite solar cell with a power-to-weight ratio of 10 W g−1 and a power conversion efficiency exceeding 30% under ambient indoor illumination, enabling autonomous operation across diverse lighting conditions. Human studies demonstrate strong correlations between EBC and blood lactate levels during exercise and carbohydrate intake, enabling real-time tracking of metabolic fluctuations. EBClite provides a sustainable, user-friendly platform for continuous respiratory and metabolic monitoring. WORLD NATIONAL BUSINESS CRYPTOCURRENCY SCIENCE TECHNOLOGY HEALTH SOCCER TENNIS FORMULA_1 ENTERTAINMENT OTHER


Optimizing biodiversity, multifunctionality and yield when transitioning to organic farming
Source: Nature    Published: 2026-03-16 10:34:38

Thank you for visiting nature.com. You are using a browser version with limited support for CSS. To obtain the best experience, we recommend you use a more up to date browser (or turn off compatibility mode in Internet Explorer). In the meantime, to ensure continued support, we are displaying the site without styles and JavaScript. Advertisement Nature Sustainability (2026)Cite this article Agricultural intensification increases crop production but often causes declines in soil biodiversity and functioning, thus threatening long-term environmental sustainability. To buffer these impacts, environmental policies aim to increase the amount of organic farming; however, this potentially compromises yield and nitrogen and phosphorus availability. Given such complexities, we evaluated the overall impacts of shifting agricultural management by comparing the effects of conventional versus organic farming on soil biodiversity (richness of 6 faunal and microbial groups), functioning (21 indicators) and crop yield on 179 global croplands across a wide range of ecological contexts, including contrasting levels of soil degradation. High crop yields did not necessarily trade off against soil biodiversity, ecosystem multifunctionality or nitrogen availability, with often positive correlations found between them. Yield, biodiversity and functioning variables showed a broad range of values within and between conventional and organic sites. Despite this large variation, we found that landscapes with 50% organic agriculture could optimize crop yields, biodiversity and multifunctionality. Our findings provide guidelines for sustainable agriculture, showing how prioritizing the transition to organic farming in moderately to highly degraded soils would maximize its benefits while minimizing yield loss. L.G.-V. performed data analysis, interpreted the results, and wrote the first draft of the paper, which was revised by the rest of the authors. P.S.-C. conducted laboratory analyses and statistical processing. S.L. contributed to conceptualization and funding acquisition. B.G., V.O., G.B., A.C., M.J., M.H., R.d.G. and L.P. carried out laboratory analyses. N.S., M.Z. and C.K. provided satellite remote sensing data and results. Fieldwork was conducted by M.P.B., I.A., J.B.D.L., L.D., F.G.-O., H.H., D.J., L.D.O.M., K.S., X.S., R.T., T.V.D.S., T.Z., K.R. and T.P. C.Y. served as project manager. S.S. conceived the idea, provided guidance on data analysis, writing and supervision of the study. All authors reviewed and approved the final version of the paper. Publisher's note Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations. Relationship between soil multidiversity (a compound index based on the scaled richness of six organism groups), multifunctionality (a compound index based on 21 uncorrelated soil functions), crop yield, N limitation (total available N), and N loss (retention of N fertilizer and abundance of denitrification genes [sum of the number of copies of nirK and nirS genes]) in highly degraded croplands (n = 63). Lines show model predictions ± 95% confidence intervals. Relationships are based on two-sided Generalized Linear Mixed Models including country as a random effect, as well as management × the variable included in the X axis in each panel as fixed effects (statistical results [t-values and their associated significance level: ns = non-significant; P > 0.05; * = P < 0.05] are shown). No adjustments for multiple comparisons were applied. Separate regression lines (brown for conventional and green for organic management) are shown. Relationship between soil multidiversity (a compound index based on the scaled richness of six organism groups), multifunctionality (a compound index based on 21 uncorrelated soil functions), crop yield, N limitation (total available N), and N loss (retention of N fertilizer and abundance of denitrification genes [sum of the number of copies of nirK and nirS genes]) in lightly degraded croplands (n = 55). Lines show model predictions ± 95% confidence intervals. Relationships are based on two-sided Generalized Linear Mixed Models including country as a random effect, as well as management × the variable included in the X axis in each panel as fixed effects (statistical results [t-values and their associated significance level: ns = non-significant; P > 0.05; * = P < 0.05] are shown). No adjustments for multiple comparisons were applied. Separate regression lines (brown for conventional and green for organic management) are shown. Relationship between soil multidiversity (a compound index based on the scaled richness of six organism groups), multifunctionality (a compound index based on 21 uncorrelated soil functions), crop yield, N limitation (total available N), and N loss (retention of N fertilizer and abundance of denitrification genes [sum of the number of copies of nirK and nirS genes]) in wheat croplands (n = 42). Lines show model predictions ± 95% confidence intervals. Relationships are based on two-sided Generalized Linear Mixed Models including country as a random effect, as well as soil degradation level and management × the variable included in the X axis in each panel as fixed effects (statistical results [t-values and their associated significance level: ns = non-significant; P > 0.05; * = P < 0.05] are shown for the variable in the X axis and its interaction with management). No adjustments for multiple comparisons were applied. Separate regression lines (brown for conventional and green for organic management) are shown. (A) Standardized values (range 0-1; mean +/- SE) for multifunctionality (brown), soil multidiversity (“biodiversity”, purple) and crop yield (green), for the 20 “best” sites (largest values of the sum of these three aspects; n = 20) and the 20 “worst” sites (lowest values of their sum; n = 20). Values for the “average” sites (neither best nor worst; n = 139) and all of our sites (“total general”; n = 179) together, are shown to ease comparison. (B) Proportion of farms with different sustainable practices other than the conventional/organic dichotomy: no-till (ploughing = 0), addition of organic manure (organic fertilization = 1) or crop rotation (1 = yes, 0 = no). 72% of our study sites (n = 129) were ploughed frequently, 93% of our sites (n = 166) received some sort of organic manure over the last 5 years, and 93% of our sites (n = 166) shifted cash crops regarding the previous season. No differences in the proportion of these practices were observed for the “best”, “average”, and “worst” sites. (C) Values of the “fine-tuned” management scores for the “best”, “average”, “worst” and “all” sites, as defined above (mean +/- SE; N=20-179) (above). The “fine-tuned” management scores was calculated by assigning a value = 1 to conventional fields, and 5 to organic, and then adding extra points if the farmer either used organic fertilizers (+1), practiced no-till agriculture (+1) or implemented crop rotation (+1). Thus, our “fine-tuned” management ranges between 1 (conventional farming with no regenerative practices) to 8 (organic farming with all three regenerative practices implemented). Management scores range between 1-8, corresponding to less sustainable to more sustainable practices. The numbers in brackets are the proportion of conventional (versus organic) sites in each category. Simulated landscapes (using observed field data) using different scenarios to those presented in the main text: A) using 10 sites instead of 5 to build the landscapes; B) focusing only on maximizing crop yield regardless of soil diversity or functioning, C) focusing only on maximizing soil biodiversity, D) focusing only on maximizing soil functioning, and E) considering stakeholder´s preferences by weighting the different nature contributions to people (NCP; average of all functions related to each NCP) instead of giving each NCP an equal weight. All soil degradation levels (low, moderate and high) are considered together, with the optimal share of conventional (brown) vs organic (green) management shown. Relative interaction index (RII) for the richness of the different organisms (A) and soil functions (B) measured in croplands with low level of soil degradation (n=55). RII (mean ± standard error) was calculated for each of the 10 pairs of conventional vs organic, selecting the respective soil degradation level. Negative RIIs show higher richness of organisms/soil functions levels in organic vs conventional agriculture, whereas positive RIIs show the contrary. Asterisks indicate significant differences (two-sided Wilcoxon signed-rank tests; P < 0.05; no adjustments for multiple comparisons were applied) for each organism/soil function. (C) Simulated landscapes (using observed field data) maximizing ecosystem multifunctionality, multidiversity and crop yield at the landscape (5 pooled sites) scale in croplands with low level of soil degradation. Different colours show the share of conventional (brown) vs organic (green) management required to maximize soil multidiversity, multifunctionality and crop yield across selected sites. Relative interaction index (RII) for the richness of the different organisms (A) and soil functions (B) measured in croplands with high level of soil degradation(n=62). RII (mean ± standard error) was calculated for each of the 10 pairs of conventional vs organic, selecting the respective soil degradation level. Negative RIIs show higher richness of organisms/soil functions levels in organic vs conventional agriculture, whereas positive RIIs show the contrary. Asterisks indicate significant differences(two-sided Wilcoxon signed-rank tests; P < 0.05; no adjustments for multiple comparisons were applied)for each organism/soil function. (C) Simulated landscapes (using observed field data) maximizing ecosystem multifunctionality, multidiversity and crop yield at the landscape (5 pooled sites) scale in highly degraded croplands. Different colours show the share of conventional (brown) vs organic (green) management required to maximize soil multidiversity, multifunctionality and crop yield across selected sites. Relative interaction index (RII) for the richness of the different organisms measured in croplands of European (five countries) and non-European regions (three countries). RII (mean ± standard error) was calculated for each of the 10 pairs of conventional vs organic by region, irrespective of their soil degradation level. Negative RIIs show higher richness of organisms levels in organic vs conventional agriculture, whereas positive RIIs show the contrary. Asterisks indicate significant differences (two-sided Wilcoxon signed-rank tests; P < 0.05; no adjustments for multiple comparisons were applied) for the contrasts shown in both panels. Relationship between the multidiversity index calculated for all biodiversity groups (prokaryotes, eukaryotes, fungi and mesofauna) and the multidiversity index including only prokaryotes, eukaryotes and fungi for European croplands (n = 110), differentiating between conventional (C) and organic management (O) (brown and green circles, respectively). All variables are standardized using Z-scores calculated for each of the eight study regions. Filled circles represent each sampling point, and Spearman's rank correlation coefficients (ρ) are shown, with asterisks indicating significant correlations (two-sided Spearman tests; P < 0.05; no adjustments for multiple comparisons were applied). Simulated landscapes (using observed field data) maximizing ecosystem multifunctionality, multidiversity and crop yield at the landscape (5 pooled sites) scale in each study site (European and non-European regions). Different colours show the share of conventional (brown) vs organic (green) management required to maximize simultaneously soil multidiversity, multifunctionality and crop yield across these 5-site landscapes.
